note_data.title: Economic growth and sustainability
keyPoints: The basic economic problem is scarcity - wants are unlimited and resources are finite, so choices have to be made
Opportunity cost is the value of the next best alternative forgone when making a choice
Economic agents (consumers, producers, governments) all face opportunity cost when making decisions with finite resources
Production decisions involve three key questions: what to produce, how to produce it, and for whom to produce it
Ceteris paribus assumes all other things are held constant when analyzing economic relationships
Making decisions at the margin means considering what the next step or action means for additional cost or benefit
The law of diminishing marginal returns states that each extra unit consumed gives less utility
Short run has at least one fixed factor of production, long run has all factors variable, very long run includes external variables like government rules and technology
Positive statements are objective and can be tested with factual evidence
Normative statements are subjective, based on value judgements and opinion rather than factual evidence

definitions.definition: The value of the next best alternative forgone
A key assumption made in economics that assumes all other things are held constant or are equal
Each extra unit of a good or service consumed gives the consumer less utility
Objective statements that can be tested with factual evidence, and can consequently be rejected or accepted
Statements based on value judgements that are subjective and based on opinion rather than factual evidence

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Give advantages AND disadvantages, then reach a justified conclusion
Present different viewpoints with evidence, show understanding of complexity

modelAnswer.question: Explain why economic growth may not always lead to improved living standards. [6 marks]
modelAnswer.answer: Economic growth may not improve living standards because the benefits may be unequally distributed, with most gains going to high-income groups while the poor see little improvement. Growth may also come at environmental costs such as pollution and resource depletion, reducing quality of life and future productive capacity. Additionally, if population growth exceeds economic growth, real GDP per capita may fall despite overall economic expansion. Finally, growth focused on capital goods rather than consumer goods may not immediately improve current living standards, though it may benefit future generations.
markBreakdown: Mark 2: Explanation of how inequality prevents improvement
Mark 3: Environmental costs reducing quality of life
Mark 4: Population growth exceeding economic growth
Mark 5: Capital vs consumer goods distinction
Mark 6: Clear examples or development of points

commonMistakes: Confusing opportunity cost with the original price paid rather than the value of the next best alternative
Not recognizing that positive statements use words like 'will' and 'is' while normative statements use 'should'
Failing to apply ceteris paribus assumption when analyzing economic effects
examinerTips: Look for words such as 'will' and 'is' to identify positive statements
Look for words such as 'should' and suggestions that one action is more credible than another to identify normative statements
formulas: Economic growth rate = (Real GDP year 2 - Real GDP year 1) / Real GDP year 1 × 100
Real GDP per capita = Real GDP / Population
HDI = (Life expectancy index + Education index + Income index) / 3
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Kuznets Curve showing relationship between economic development (x-axis) and income inequality (y-axis) - inequality initially rises then falls as economy develops, forming an inverted U-shape
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (6 marks) — they asked students to explain the difference between economic growth and economic development with reference to sustainability
N/D 2022 P1 Q2 (4 marks) — required analysis of how rapid economic growth might conflict with environmental sustainability
M/J 2022 P2 Q5(b) (8 marks) — evaluate whether developing countries should prioritize economic growth over environmental protection
N/D 2021 P1 Q3 (5 marks) — explain why sustainable development is important for future generations, using examples

workedExample.question: Explain why economic growth may not always lead to sustainable development. (6 marks)
steps: Step 1: Define economic growth as an increase in real GDP over time
Step 2: Define sustainable development as meeting present needs without compromising future generations' ability to meet their needs
Step 3: Explain how growth can harm environment through pollution, resource depletion, and habitat destruction
Step 4: Give specific examples like deforestation in Brazil or air pollution in China
Step 5: Explain how this threatens future economic capacity and quality of life
workedExample.answer: Economic growth measures the increase in real GDP over time, while sustainable development means meeting current needs without compromising future generations. Economic growth may harm sustainability because rapid industrialization often leads to environmental degradation. For example, manufacturing growth can increase air and water pollution, while agricultural expansion may cause deforestation and soil erosion. Resource extraction for growth depletes finite natural resources like oil and minerals. This environmental damage threatens future economic capacity by reducing agricultural productivity, increasing health costs, and limiting tourism potential, making growth unsustainable in the long term.
workedExample.markScheme: 2 marks for definitions, 2 marks for explaining the conflict, 2 marks for relevant examples or analysis of long-term consequences

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused economic growth with economic development' — always distinguish between GDP growth and improvements in living standards when discussing sustainability
Examiner Report 2022: 'Weak responses failed to consider long-term consequences' — always explain how current economic decisions affect future generations in sustainability questions
Examiner Report 2021: 'Students gave vague environmental examples' — use specific, real-world examples of environmental costs like named pollutants or actual deforestation statistics
note_data.keyDiagram: Draw a graph with two curves over time: 'Economic Output' rising steeply upward, and 'Environmental Quality' declining downward, showing the trade-off. Label the x-axis 'Time' and y-axis 'Level'. Add a dotted horizontal line labeled 'Sustainability Threshold' where environmental quality becomes critically low. Mark the intersection point as 'Crisis Point' where continued growth becomes impossible due to environmental collapse.
note_data.quickRecap: Economics deals with scarcity where unlimited wants meet finite resources, requiring choices that involve opportunity cost. Economic analysis uses ceteris paribus assumptions and marginal decision-making. Distinguish between testable positive statements and opinion-based normative statements when evaluating economic claims.
